tipRules.tip: Wheeler: In heavy current, use a DT20 even when fish are in 12-18 ft — gets down quicker and stays in the strike zone longer than a shallower-running crank. Cranking catches the biggest fish via reaction bites

Wheeler: An old-school TX-rigged 6-inch worm on ledges is something people forget about — you can go behind other anglers and get bites when fish won't eat a crankbait or are not grouped up. Use 3/8-oz tungsten, 4/0 hook, 14-lb fluorocarbon

Wheeler: A 1-oz spinnerbait (big Colorado/Willow combo) with a 4-inch paddle tail swimbait trailer imitates a bigger-profile gizzard shad — gets fewer bites but the right bites from bigger fish. Cast upstream and bump it down with the current

knowledge.insight: Wheeler: In heavy TN River current, fish don't set up consistently — sometimes behind a stump, sometimes on the front edge of the current. The pattern is never always the same, so the goal is to find as many places as possible
Wheeler: Fish trying to move offshore can get held up by the shad spawn in 4-8 ft of water. In years with floods and cold fronts, the post-spawn transition can run behind schedule
Wheeler: At Pickwick with heavy current, fish were in 10-30 ft with no depth consistency — depth depended on the area of the lake and the structure in that area
